sourceRefs.fallbackText: This chapter describes some things you’ve learned about already in more detail, and adds some new things as well.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The list data type has some more methods. Here are all of the methods of list objects:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The list data type has some more methods. Here are all of the methods of list objects:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Add an item to the end of the list. Similar to a[len(a):] = [x] .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Insert an item at a given position. The first argument is the index of the element before which to insert, so a.insert(0, x) inserts at the front of the list, and a.insert(len(a), x) is equivalent to a.append(x) .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Insert an item at a given position. The first argument is the index of the element before which to insert, so a.insert(0, x) inserts at the front of the list, and a.insert(len(a), x) is equivalent to a.append(x) .

highSignalBlocks.text: Insert an item at a given position. The first argument is the index of the element before which to insert, so a.insert(0, x) inserts at the front of the list, and a.insert(len(a), x) is equivalent to a.append(x) .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Remove the first item from the list whose value is equal to value . It raises a ValueError if there is no such item.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Remove the first item from the list whose value is equal to value . It raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

highSignalBlocks.text: Remove the first item from the list whose value is equal to value . It raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Remove the item at the given position in the list, and return it. If no index is specified, a.pop() removes and returns the last item in the list. It raises an IndexError if the list is empty or the index is outside the list range.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Remove all items from the list. Similar to del a[:] .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Remove all items from the list. Similar to del a[:] .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return zero-based index of the first occurrence of value in the list. Raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

highSignalBlocks.text: Return zero-based index of the first occurrence of value in the list. Raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The optional arguments start and end are interpreted as in the slice notation and are used to limit the search to a particular subsequence of the list. The returned index is computed relative to the beginning of the full sequence rather than the start argument.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return the number of times value appears in the list.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Return the number of times value appears in the list.

sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> fruits = [ 'orange' , 'apple' , 'pear' , 'banana' , 'kiwi' , 'apple' , 'banana' ] >>> fruits . count ( 'apple' ) 2 >>> fruits . count ( 'tangerine' ) 0 >>> fruits . index ( 'banana' ) 3 >>> fruits . index ( 'banana'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You might have noticed that methods like insert , remove or sort that only modify the list have no return value printed – they return the default None . [ 1 ] This is a design principle for all mutable data structures in Python.
sourceRefs.textQuote: You might have noticed that methods like insert , remove or sort that only modify the list have no return value printed – they return the default None . [ 1 ] This is a design principle for all mutable data structures in

highSignalBlocks.text: You might have noticed that methods like insert , remove or sort that only modify the list have no return value printed – they return the default None . [ 1 ] This is a design principle for all mutable data structures in Python.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another thing you might notice is that not all data can be sorted or compared. For instance, [None, 'hello', 10] doesn’t sort because integers can’t be compared to strings and None can’t be compared to other types. Also, there are some types that don’t have a defined ordering relation. For example, 3+4j < 5+7j isn’t a valid comparison.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another thing you might notice is that not all data can be sorted or compared. For instance, [None, 'hello', 10] doesn’t sort because integers can’t be compared to strings and None can’t be compared to other types. Also,

For instance, [None, 'hello', 10] doesn’t sort because integers can’t be compared to strings and None can’t be compared to other types. Also, there are some types that don’t have a defined ordering relation. For example, 3+4j < 5+7j isn’t a valid comparison.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The list methods make it very easy to use a list as a stack, where the last element added is the first element retrieved (“last-in, first-out”). To add an item to the top of the stack, use append() . To retrieve an item from the top of the stack, use pop() without an explicit index. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The list methods make it very easy to use a list as a stack, where the last element added is the first element retrieved (“last-in, first-out”). To add an item to the top of the stack, use append() . To retrieve an item

highSignalBlocks.text: The list methods make it very easy to use a list as a stack, where the last element added is the first element retrieved (“last-in, first-out”). To add an item to the top of the stack, use append() . To retrieve an item from the top of the stack, use pop() without an explicit index. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> stack = [ 3 , 4 , 5 ] >>> stack . append ( 6 ) >>> stack . append ( 7 ) >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6, 7] >>> stack . pop () 7 >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6] >>> stack . pop () 6 >>> stack . pop () 5 >>> stack [3, 4]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> stack = [ 3 , 4 , 5 ] >>> stack . append ( 6 ) >>> stack . append ( 7 ) >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6, 7] >>> stack . pop () 7 >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6] >>> stack . pop () 6 >>> stack . pop () 5 >>> stack [3, 4]

highSignalBlocks.text: >>> stack = [ 3 , 4 , 5 ] >>> stack . append ( 6 ) >>> stack . append ( 7 ) >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6, 7] >>> stack . pop () 7 >>> stack [3, 4, 5, 6] >>> stack . pop () 6 >>> stack . pop () 5 >>> stack [3, 4]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is also possible to use a list as a queue, where the first element added is the first element retrieved (“first-in, first-out”); however, lists are not efficient for this purpose. While appends and pops from the end of list are fast, doing inserts or pops from the beginning of a list is slow (because all of the other elements have to be shifted by one).
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is also possible to use a list as a queue, where the first element added is the first element retrieved (“first-in, first-out”); however, lists are not efficient for this purpose. While appends and pops from the end o

highSignalBlocks.text: It is also possible to use a list as a queue, where the first element added is the first element retrieved (“first-in, first-out”); however, lists are not efficient for this purpose. While appends and pops from the end of list are fast, doing inserts or pops from the beginning of a list is slow (because all of the other elements have to be shifted by one).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To implement a queue, use collections.deque which was designed to have fast appends and pops from both ends. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To implement a queue, use collections.deque which was designed to have fast appends and pops from both ends. For example:

highSignalBlocks.text: To implement a queue, use collections.deque which was designed to have fast appends and pops from both ends. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> from collections import deque >>> queue = deque ([ "Eric" , "John" , "Michael" ]) >>> queue . append ( "Terry" ) # Terry arrives >>> queue . append ( "Graham" ) # Graham arrives >>> queue . popleft () # The first to arrive now leaves 'Eric' >>> queue . popleft () # The second to arrive now leaves 'John' >>> queue # Remaining queue in order of arrival deq
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> from collections import deque >>> queue = deque ([ "Eric" , "John" , "Michael" ]) >>> queue . append ( "Terry" ) # Terry arrives >>> queue . append ( "Graham" ) # Graham arrives >>> queue . popleft () # The first to

highSignalBlocks.text: >>> from collections import deque >>> queue = deque ([ "Eric" , "John" , "Michael" ]) >>> queue . append ( "Terry" ) # Terry arrives >>> queue . append ( "Graham" ) # Graham arrives >>> queue . popleft () # The first to arrive now leaves 'Eric' >>> queue . popleft () # The second to arrive now leaves 'John' >>> queue # Remaining queue in order of arrival deque(['Michael', 'Terry', 'Graham'])

sourceRefs.fallbackText: List comprehensions provide a concise way to create lists. Common applications are to make new lists where each element is the result of some operations applied to each member of another sequence or iterable, or to create a subsequence of those elements that satisfy a certain condition.
sourceRefs.textQuote: List comprehensions provide a concise way to create lists. Common applications are to make new lists where each element is the result of some operations applied to each member of another sequence or iterable, or to creat

highSignalBlocks.text: List comprehensions provide a concise way to create lists. Common applications are to make new lists where each element is the result of some operations applied to each member of another sequence or iterable, or to create a subsequence of those elements that satisfy a certain condition.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For example, assume we want to create a list of squares, like:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For example, assume we want to create a list of squares, like:

highSignalBlocks.text: For example, assume we want to create a list of squares, like:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> squares = [] >>> for x in range ( 10 ): ... squares . append ( x ** 2 ) ... >>> squares [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> squares = [] >>> for x in range ( 10 ): ... squares . append ( x ** 2 ) ... >>> squares [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81]

highSignalBlocks.text: >>> squares = [] >>> for x in range ( 10 ): ... squares . append ( x ** 2 ) ... >>> squares [0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A list comprehension consists of brackets containing an expression followed by a for clause, then zero or more for or if clauses. The result will be a new list resulting from evaluating the expression in the context of the for and if clauses which follow it. For example, this listcomp combines the elements of two lists if they are not equal:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We saw that lists and strings have many common properties, such as indexing and slicing operations. They are two examples of sequence data types (see Sequence Types — list, tuple, range ). Since Python is an evolving language, other sequence data types may be added. There is also another standard sequence data type: the tuple .

There is also another standard sequence data type: the tuple .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As you see, on output tuples are always enclosed in parentheses, so that nested tuples are interpreted correctly; they may be input with or without surrounding parentheses, although often parentheses are necessary anyway (if the tuple is part of a larger expression). It is not possible to assign to the individual items of a tuple, however it is possible to c
sourceRefs.textQuote: As you see, on output tuples are always enclosed in parentheses, so that nested tuples are interpreted correctly; they may be input with or without surrounding parentheses, although often parentheses are necessary anyway

highSignalBlocks.text: As you see, on output tuples are always enclosed in parentheses, so that nested tuples are interpreted correctly; they may be input with or without surrounding parentheses, although often parentheses are necessary anyway (if the tuple is part of a larger expression). It is not possible to assign to the individual items of a tuple, however it is possible to create tuples which contain mutable objects, such as lists.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Though tuples may seem similar to lists, they are often used in different situations and for different purposes. Tuples are immutable , and usually contain a heterogeneous sequence of elements that are accessed via unpacking (see later in this section) or indexing (or even by attribute in the case of namedtuples ). Lists are mutable , and their elements are
sourceRefs.textQuote: Though tuples may seem similar to lists, they are often used in different situations and for different purposes. Tuples are immutable , and usually contain a heterogeneous sequence of elements that are accessed via unpac

highSignalBlocks.text: Though tuples may seem similar to lists, they are often used in different situations and for different purposes. Tuples are immutable , and usually contain a heterogeneous sequence of elements that are accessed via unpacking (see later in this section) or indexing (or even by attribute in the case of namedtuples ). Lists are mutable , and their elements are usually homogeneous and are accessed by iterating over the list.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A special problem is the construction of tuples containing 0 or 1 items: the syntax has some extra quirks to accommodate these. Empty tuples are constructed by an empty pair of parentheses; a tuple with one item is constructed by following a value with a comma (it is not sufficient to enclose a single value in parentheses). Ugly, but effective. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The statement t = 12345, 54321, 'hello!' is an example of tuple packing : the values 12345 , 54321 and 'hello!' are packed together in a tuple. The reverse operation is also possible:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is called, appropriately enough, sequence unpacking and works for any sequence on the right-hand side. Sequence unpacking requires that there are as many variables on the left side of the equals sign as there are elements in the sequence. Note that multiple assignment is really just a combination of tuple packing and sequence unpacking.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Python also includes a data type for sets . A set is an unordered collection with no duplicate elements. Basic uses include membership testing and eliminating duplicate entries. Set objects also support mathematical operations like union, intersection, difference, and symmetric difference.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Python also includes a data type for sets . A set is an unordered collection with no duplicate elements. Basic uses include membership testing and eliminating duplicate entries. Set objects also support mathematical oper

highSignalBlocks.text: Python also includes a data type for sets . A set is an unordered collection with no duplicate elements. Basic uses include membership testing and eliminating duplicate entries. Set objects also support mathematical operations like union, intersection, difference, and symmetric difference.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Curly braces or the set() function can be used to create sets. Note: to create an empty set you have to use set() , not {} ; the latter creates an empty dictionary, a data structure that we discuss in the next section.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Curly braces or the set() function can be used to create sets. Note: to create an empty set you have to use set() , not {} ; the latter creates an empty dictionary, a data structure that we discuss in the next section.

highSignalBlocks.text: Curly braces or the set() function can be used to create sets. Note: to create an empty set you have to use set() , not {} ; the latter creates an empty dictionary, a data structure that we discuss in the next section.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Because sets are unordered, iterating over them or printing them can produce the elements in a different order than you expect.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Because sets are unordered, iterating over them or printing them can produce the elements in a different order than you expect.

highSignalBlocks.text: Because sets are unordered, iterating over them or printing them can produce the elements in a different order than you expect.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here is a brief demonstration:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here is a brief demonstration:

highSignalBlocks.text: Here is a brief demonstration:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> basket = { 'apple' , 'orange' , 'apple' , 'pear' , 'orange' , 'banana' } >>> print ( basket ) # show that duplicates have been removed {'orange', 'banana', 'pear', 'apple'} >>> 'orange' in basket # fast membership testing True >>> 'crabgrass' in basket False >>> # Demonstrate set operations on unique letters from two words >>> >>> a = set ( 'abracadabra'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> basket = { 'apple' , 'orange' , 'apple' , 'pear' , 'orange' , 'banana' } >>> print ( basket ) # show that duplicates have been removed {'orange', 'banana', 'pear', 'apple'} >>> 'orange' in basket # fast membership te

highSignalBlocks.text: >>> basket = { 'apple' , 'orange' , 'apple' , 'pear' , 'orange' , 'banana' } >>> print ( basket ) # show that duplicates have been removed {'orange', 'banana', 'pear', 'apple'} >>> 'orange' in basket # fast membership testing True >>> 'crabgrass' in basket False >>> # Demonstrate set operations on unique letters from two words >>> >>> a = set ( 'abracadabra' ) >>> b = set ( 'alacazam' ) >>> a # unique letters in a {'a', 'r', 'b', 'c', 'd'} >>> a - b # letters in a but not in b {'r', 'd', 'b'} >>> a | b # letters in a or b or both {'a', 'c', 'r', 'd', 'b', 'm', 'z', 'l'} >>> a & b # letters in both a and b {'a', 'c'} >>> a ^ b # letters in a or b but not both {'r', 'd', 'b', 'm', 'z', 'l'}

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another useful data type built into Python is the dictionary (see Mapping Types — dict ). Dictionaries are sometimes found in other languages as “associative memories” or “associative arrays”. Unlike sequences, which are indexed by a range of numbers, dictionaries are indexed by keys , which can be any immutable type; strings and numbers can always be keys.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another useful data type built into Python is the dictionary (see Mapping Types — dict ). Dictionaries are sometimes found in other languages as “associative memories” or “associative arrays”. Unlike sequences, which are

highSignalBlocks.text: Another useful data type built into Python is the dictionary (see Mapping Types — dict ). Dictionaries are sometimes found in other languages as “associative memories” or “associative arrays”. Unlike sequences, which are indexed by a range of numbers, dictionaries are indexed by keys , which can be any immutable type; strings and numbers can always be keys. Tuples can be used as keys if they contain only strings, numbers, or tuples; if a tuple contains any mutable object either directly or indirectly, it cannot be used as a key. You can’t use lists as keys, since lists can be modified in place using index assignments, slice assignments, or methods like append() and extend() .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is best to think of a dictionary as a set of key: value pairs, with the requirement that the keys are unique (within one dictionary). A pair of braces creates an empty dictionary: {} . Placing a comma-separated list of key:value pairs within the braces adds initial key:value pairs to the dictionary; this is also the way dictionaries are written on output.
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is best to think of a dictionary as a set of key: value pairs, with the requirement that the keys are unique (within one dictionary). A pair of braces creates an empty dictionary: {} . Placing a comma-separated list o

highSignalBlocks.text: It is best to think of a dictionary as a set of key: value pairs, with the requirement that the keys are unique (within one dictionary). A pair of braces creates an empty dictionary: {} . Placing a comma-separated list of key:value pairs within the braces adds initial key:value pairs to the dictionary; this is also the way dictionaries are written on output.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The main operations on a dictionary are storing a value with some key and extracting the value given the key. It is also possible to delete a key:value pair with del . If you store using a key that is already in use, the old value associated with that key is forgotten.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The main operations on a dictionary are storing a value with some key and extracting the value given the key. It is also possible to delete a key:value pair with del . If you store using a key that is already in use, the

highSignalBlocks.text: The main operations on a dictionary are storing a value with some key and extracting the value given the key. It is also possible to delete a key:value pair with del . If you store using a key that is already in use, the old value associated with that key is forgotten.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Extracting a value for a non-existent key by subscripting ( d[key] ) raises a KeyError . To avoid getting this error when trying to access a possibly non-existent key, use the get() method instead, which returns None (or a specified default value) if the key is not in the dictionary.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Extracting a value for a non-existent key by subscripting ( d[key] ) raises a KeyError . To avoid getting this error when trying to access a possibly non-existent key, use the get() method instead, which returns None (or

To avoid getting this error when trying to access a possibly non-existent key, use the get() method instead, which returns None (or a specified default value) if the key is not in the dictionary.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Performing list(d) on a dictionary returns a list of all the keys used in the dictionary, in insertion order (if you want it sorted, just use sorted(d) instead). To check whether a single key is in the dictionary, use the in keyword.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When looping through dictionaries, the key and corresponding value can be retrieved at the same time using the items() method.

It is {1} .' . format ( q , a )) ... What is your name? It is lancelot. What is your quest? It is the holy grail. What is your favorite color? It is blue.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Using set() on a sequence eliminates duplicate elements. The use of sorted() in combination with set() over a sequence is an idiomatic way to loop over unique elements of the sequence in sorted order.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The conditions used in while and if statements can contain any operators, not just comparisons.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The conditions used in while and if statements can contain any operators, not just comparisons.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The comparison operators in and not in are membership tests that determine whether a value is in (or not in) a container. The operators is and is not compare whether two objects are really the same object. All comparison operators have the same priority, which is lower than that of all numerical operators.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The comparison operators in and not in are membership tests that determine whether a value is in (or not in) a container. The operators is and is not compare whether two objects are really the same object. All comparison

highSignalBlocks.text: The comparison operators in and not in are membership tests that determine whether a value is in (or not in) a container. The operators is and is not compare whether two objects are really the same object. All comparison operators have the same priority, which is lower than that of all numerical operators.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Comparisons can be chained. For example, a < b == c tests whether a is less than b and moreover b equals c .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Comparisons can be chained. For example, a < b == c tests whether a is less than b and moreover b equals c .

highSignalBlocks.text: Comparisons can be chained. For example, a < b == c tests whether a is less than b and moreover b equals c .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Comparisons may be combined using the Boolean operators and and or , and the outcome of a comparison (or of any other Boolean expression) may be negated with not . These have lower priorities than comparison operators; between them, not has the highest priority and or the lowest, so that A and not B or C is equivalent to (A and (not B)) or C . As always, par
sourceRefs.textQuote: Comparisons may be combined using the Boolean operators and and or , and the outcome of a comparison (or of any other Boolean expression) may be negated with not . These have lower priorities than comparison operators; b

highSignalBlocks.text: Comparisons may be combined using the Boolean operators and and or , and the outcome of a comparison (or of any other Boolean expression) may be negated with not . These have lower priorities than comparison operators; between them, not has the highest priority and or the lowest, so that A and not B or C is equivalent to (A and (not B)) or C . As always, parentheses can be used to express the desired composition.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Boolean operators and and or are so-called short-circuit operators: their arguments are evaluated from left to right, and evaluation stops as soon as the outcome is determined. For example, if A and C are true but B is false, A and B and C does not evaluate the expression C . When used as a general value and not as a Boolean, the return value of a short-
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Boolean operators and and or are so-called short-circuit operators: their arguments are evaluated from left to right, and evaluation stops as soon as the outcome is determined. For example, if A and C are true but B

highSignalBlocks.text: The Boolean operators and and or are so-called short-circuit operators: their arguments are evaluated from left to right, and evaluation stops as soon as the outcome is determined. For example, if A and C are true but B is false, A and B and C does not evaluate the expression C . When used as a general value and not as a Boolean, the return value of a short-circuit operator is the last evaluated argument.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is possible to assign the result of a comparison or other Boolean expression to a variable. For example,
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is possible to assign the result of a comparison or other Boolean expression to a variable. For example,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that in Python, unlike C, assignment inside expressions must be done explicitly with the walrus operator := . This avoids a common class of problems encountered in C programs: typing = in an expression when == was intended.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that in Python, unlike C, assignment inside expressions must be done explicitly with the walrus operator := . This avoids a common class of problems encountered in C programs: typing = in an expression when == was i

highSignalBlocks.text: Note that in Python, unlike C, assignment inside expressions must be done explicitly with the walrus operator := . This avoids a common class of problems encountered in C programs: typing = in an expression when == was intended.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Sequence objects typically may be compared to other objects with the same sequence type. The comparison uses lexicographical ordering: first the first two items are compared, and if they differ this determines the outcome of the comparison; if they are equal, the next two items are compared, and so on, until either sequence is exhausted. sourceRefs.textQuote: Sequence objects typically may be compared to other objects with the same sequence type. The comparison uses lexicographical ordering: first the first two items are compared, and if they differ this determines the outcom

highSignalBlocks.text: Sequence objects typically may be compared to other objects with the same sequence type. The comparison uses lexicographical ordering: first the first two items are compared, and if they differ this determines the outcome of the comparison; if they are equal, the next two items are compared, and so on, until either sequence is exhausted. If two items to be compared are themselves sequences of the same type, the lexicographical comparison is carried out recursively. If all items of two sequences compare equal, the sequences are considered equal. If one sequence is an initial sub-sequence of the other, the shorter sequence is the smaller (lesser) one. Lexicographical ordering for strings uses the Unicode code point number to order individual characters. Some examples of comparisons between sequences of the same type:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that comparing objects of different types with < or > is legal provided that the objects have appropriate comparison methods. For example, mixed numeric types are compared according to their numeric value, so 0 equals 0.0, etc. Otherwise, rather than providing an arbitrary ordering, the interpreter will raise a TypeError exception.
